Exhibit 01 / Soccer Culture Project

Manchester United and Soccer Culture in England

Project overview and reading guide for a class exhibit on Manchester United and English soccer culture.

Oakland University · Social Movements & Sports · 4 sections

Match Programme № 001
England × Man United
History · Culture · Supporters

Manchester United and Soccer Culture in England

#Project Overview

This site is a small digital exhibit produced for a class project at Oakland University. It looks at two intertwined subjects: the broader culture of soccer (football) in England, and the history, identity, and global reach of Manchester United Football Club. The two halves are meant to be read together — Manchester United is impossible to understand without the country and city that shaped it, and English soccer is hard to discuss without a club like United at the table.

We treat the topic the way a museum exhibit would: short, focused sections, careful sourcing, and a layout that invites browsing as much as reading.

#How to Use This Site

Use the navigation at the top of every page to move between the four parts of the exhibit. On longer pages, an "On this page" panel on the right (or above the article on small screens) jumps directly to a section. Every factual claim is meant to be backed by a source listed on the Sources page; if you find one that is not, please open an issue on the project repository.

The site is fully static. It works without JavaScript, without a backend, and without any third-party fonts or analytics.

#Project Sections

  • Soccer in England — the social, political, economic, and media context that makes English football what it is.
  • History of Manchester United — founding, pivotal moments, key players, stadium history, supporter culture, and the club's place in global soccer.
  • Sources — citations, references, and image credits for everything else on the site.

#Team Responsibilities

This project was researched and written by three students. Each member is responsible for a defined slice of the exhibit:

  • Evan Pardon — site scaffold, the Soccer in England page, political landscape, economic structures, fan culture and traditions, media representation, and the transfer-milestones section on the United page.
  • Jared Ekstrom — historical development, founding and early history, legendary or influential players, stadium history and significance.
  • SavorySheikah — World Cup connections, fan base and supporter culture, political and social events connected to the team, contributions to national and global soccer culture, and regional and ethnic identities.
